data.title: Mueller Water: Valve Wide Open For Growth As Flow Solutions Power 80% Rally
data.description:
Mueller Water Products, Inc. (NYSE:MWA) is set to release its earnings results in less than two weeks.

Considering the 80% rise in the share price since last October, many investors will be keeping a close eye on the upcoming earnings results to see if the company can continue its growth, particularly in the water flow solutions segment, which saw a 38.6% jump in net sales last quarter.

Even though most of the company's valuation ratios are near their 5-year highs, I believe the bull run could extend into the coming quarters, as the fundamentals are supporting this growth.

Additionally, when comparing the company to some of its industry peers, the valuation ratios don’t suggest overvaluation.

For these reasons, along with other arguments that I will discuss below, I hold a Buy rating for this stock.

Company Overview

Mueller Water Products is a US-based company that specializes in water distribution systems, including fire hydrants, valves, and other leak detection technologies, serving both municipal and industrial sectors.

The company generates revenue by manufacturing and selling these products, in addition to repairing and replacing aging water infrastructure. They also provide live monitoring services for leak detection and maintenance related to their water management solutions.

The company operates through two main segments:

  • Water Flow Solutions: this segment focuses on iron gate valves and service brass products, used for controlling the flow of water in large municipal systems.
  • Water Management Solutions: this segment focuses on fire hydrants, leak detection systems, metering solutions, and pressure management.

I considered including below a breakdown of total revenue per business segment from the latest 10-Q.

The company mainly operates in the US, however a small portion of the total revenue originated in Canada.

Year U.S. Revenue (in millions) Canada Revenue (in millions) International Revenue (in millions)
2024 $891.4 $54.1 $21.0
2023 $886.7 $59.1 $28.5

Ownership-wise, the latest 14A shows 1.4% ownership of common stock among all 18 directors and executive officers. In my view, this is not enough skin in the game.

Just as a side note, I tend to prefer companies where the management team owns more than 20% of the common stock.

Price Action

I’d like to begin by giving a brief overview of price action.

As shown in the weekly chart below, the share price has surged by over 80% since last October, and the RSI is now nearing the overbought zone.

A quick comparison with the specialty industrial machinery industry, the industrials sector, and the S&P 500 reveals that the company has outperformed all of them by a large margin.

Let’s take a closer look now at the fundamentals and find the factors driving this significant increase in share price.

Fundamentals

Before I start with fundamental analysis, a quick note.

The company's business is seasonal due to the impact of cold weather conditions. Net sales and operating income are historically the lowest in Q4 and Q1, as construction activity is restricted in the northern United States and most of Canada due to cold weather conditions.

A quick look at the income statement shows the effect of this seasonality.

For this reason, I will consider YoY variations, rather than QoQ changes.

Therefore, I included below a quarterly chart showing YoY change in revenue.

From the chart above, it seems that revenue increased in the high single digits in the last two quarters. However, what raised my attention was the significant jump in gross profit, growing by more than 30% YoY in Q1 and Q2 this year.

Furthermore, even more impressive is the improvement in operating and net income, with triple-digit growth in Q1 this year.

So, what caused these improvements in the income statement?

Well, it seems that the most significant driver was an increase in volumes, particularly in the water flow solutions segment, which saw a 38.6% rise in net sales.

This is a positive sign of recovery from inventory destocking by distributors in 2023.

However, volume growth was not the only contributing factor. The company implemented price increases across most product lines within the water flow solutions segment while keeping cost increases relatively under control. This led to an improvement of over 30% in gross margins.

Next, the balance sheet reflects a strong financial position for the company. Net debt has dropped by double digits over the past three quarters, while cash per share has risen significantly during the same period.

The company hasn't been issuing a significant amount of shares since Q2 2022. Conversely, the company has been buying back shares in Q1 and Q2 this year, despite the significant increase in the share price. In my view, this is a good sign of confidence in the current share price.

The company's liquidity ratios look really good, with $243.3 million in cash & equivalents at the end of the last quarter.

Ratio Value
Current Ratio 3.70
Quick Ratio 2.31
Cash Ratio 1.15

In regard to the debt structure, I don't see any particular red flags. The total debt of the company sits at $452.3 million, composed primarily of 4.0% Senior Notes ($450.0 million) and finance leases ($2.3 million).

Additionally, the company maintains an ABL Agreement for additional liquidity, with a borrowing capacity of up to $175.0 million, none of which is currently utilized.

Moving on to the cash flow statement, the last two quarters show a decent double-digit increase.

As mentioned, the company's business is highly seasonal during the winter. Therefore, I am not overly concerned with the negative cash flows in Q2 FY2024.

Overall, the financial statements paint a positive picture, which I believe supports the significant rise in the share price this year.

Therefore, I believe the bull run is sufficiently backed up by fundamentals.

Insiders / Activist / Institutional Buying Activity

A quick at the latest insider transactions shows a mixed picture. While there isn't any recent major selling activity by key executives, there isn't any major buying activity either.

One of the directors, Brian Healy, bought on October 7 about $24k worth of shares. However, the amount is not enough to signal anything.

Another director, Ortiz Christine, and the SVP of Water Management Solutions sold over $100k in August and May, respectively. However, I'm not too concerned about these transactions, given the time gap between them and the relatively modest amounts.

Moving on to recent activist transactions, Vanguard has been increasing their position in June, by 6%.

Finally, considering the 72% decline in the put/call ratio among 13F filers in the second quarter, it seems that the pessimistic sentiment across institutional investors has cooled off in the last quarter.

Despite a 47% rise in closed positions, indicating that some funds opted to take profits, the 57% increase in new positions is encouraging. In my view, this could suggest that institutional investors (generally) have a positive outlook on the stock.

Valuation

If you read any of my previous articles, you might be aware that I never try to estimate a fair price for a company.

My valuation analysis is quite straightforward, focusing on comparing the company's valuation ratios to its own historical range, and then to some of its peers.

Considering the bull run this year, I am not surprised to see most of the valuation ratios trading close to the 5-year maximum.

Looking at the chart above, one could infer overvaluation, however, if we compare these ratios to some of the competitors, the conclusion is quite different.

The table above is sorted in descending order by P/CF. Since the company is in the lower half of the chart (even when compared to EV/EBITDA), I have enough confidence to state that the current ratios don’t suggest overvaluation.

Risks

Despite the growth in the water flow solutions segment, the water management solutions segment experienced a decline in net sales by 15.8%, largely due to reduced demand across most product lines.

The decline in volume was partly due to customers reducing their stock in 2023, and this trend continued into 2024 for this segment, without clear signs of a turnaround.

Another risk comes from the Israel-Hamas war, which disrupted operations in the water management solutions segment. This conflict led to production delays at their Krausz facility in Israel, which manufactures pipe repair products. If the conflict escalates, this segment faces a significant risk.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I maintain a Buy rating for this stock, despite the significant increase in the share price over the past year.

In my view, the growth in revenue, gross profit, and operating income, driven by increased volumes and effective pricing strategies in the water flow solutions segment, supports the recent share price surge.

The balance sheet reflects a healthy financial position, with strong liquidity ratios and declining net debt.

However, there are risks, especially within the water management solutions segment, where declining demand and disruptions from geopolitical conflicts could impact future performance. Additionally, I would prefer to see stronger management ownership to align interests with shareholders.

data.title: Nordic Cables In A Global Market, The NKT Story
data.description:
Nordic Gems: Why NKT Plays In A Key Industry

One of the deepest and irreversible global changes we are experiencing is the energy transition. We need more energy, and we need cleaner energy. Many investors are pouring money into renewable energy companies and utilities. But another industry should not be overlooked: cable manufacturers. Here, a few main players exercise their leadership and manufacture the high-voltage and medium-low-voltage cables we all need for our grids.

After dealing with the industry leader Prysmian, (OTCPK:PRYMF) that focuses on voltage and fiber optic cables, it is time to go over one of its competitors, the Danish NKT (OTCPK:NRKBF). The name stands for "Nordiske Kabel og Traadfabrik", which means "Nordic cable and wire factory". From the name we can rightly understand that the company's main area of operations is the Baltic Sea and the countries around that area (the U.K., Germany, Poland, the Netherlands).

Northern Europe as a core market is a nice place to be in. It is a rich market with some degree of protection entrenched in the local business culture, which the World Economic Forum calls a "constructive nationalism". In particular, most of the industries here moved up the value chain, specializing in hard-to-build products. This also creates a preference to buy products from the local manufacturers, as they are perceived as higher-quality, even if sold at higher prices. This is a good environment for a local player such as NKT to exercise its leadership, especially in high-voltage cables.

NKT: An Overview

As said, NKT is a company that designs and produces power cables, with a strong focus on high-voltage cables.

The strong increase in its market has fuelled an exciting improvement in its financial data coming from the 2023 annual report, where we see rapid growth from 2019 to 2023 with revenues more than doubling from EUR 1.27B to EUR 2.57B.

The company has three main areas of operations: solutions, applications, and services. We see the business model of NKT in this image taken from the annual report, where the three business lines are shown together with the flow we see in the upper band of the picture showing what kind of resources NKT relies on to run its business and create value.

As we can see, the main business line is solutions, with services only accounting for 10% of total revenues.

There are three main drivers of its future growth: sustainability, electrification and digitalization. To prove this, NKT explains in its annual report that Europe's decarbonization and energy security targets "require an increase from around 20 GW to at least 111 GW of energy generation from offshore wind alone by 2030)". In addition, power grid operators must face increased demand from new electrical power consumers such as EVs, heat pumps, and industrial motors. As a result, NKT's forecast - taken from its annual report - is as follows:

  • Electricity consumption across Europe is expected to increase by approximately 60% between 2023 and 2030
  • Over 40% of Europe’s power grid infrastructure is over 40 years old, needing to be enhanced and/or replaced
  • Europe’s power grid owners are expected to spend at least EUR 35B per year until 2030 and up to EUR 65B per year on average until 2050
  • The EUR 23B annual spend by European power grid owners seen in recent years falls significantly short in meeting the EU’s decarbonization agenda

In this volcanic market NKT has been able to improve dramatically its order collection boosting its high-voltage cable order book from EUR 4.7B at the end of 2022 to EUR 11.3B at the end of 1H24 (without counting some frame agreement for capacity reservation already signed and totaling an additional EUR 2.5B already locked in and in their hands). No wonder that the company planned a EUR 1B investment to increase production capacity in the period from 2023 through 2027. However, there is some concentration risk in its order book. In fact, it has to be underlined that on the awarded high-voltage projects with a combined value of approximately EUR 7B in 2023 for NKT's Solution Business line, around half of it is related to one single customer, the German utility 50Hertz.

The company's leadership group has been recently selected with the majority of management having joined NKT in the last 3 to 5 years (some of them come from the main competitor Prysmian). Therefore, we can assume this team will lead the company for some time, giving the company a certain degree of stability. The CEO was replaced last year, but we will talk about this later on.

For sure, the new management followed the general conservative approach to the balance sheet. In fact, despite a strong improvement in the company's financial result, NKT decided to keep its liquidity in hand distributing no dividend in 2024.

NKT: The Market

Let's spend a few words on the high-voltage market. After all, not many are familiar with what is happening in this sector. Well, we are before an unprecedented growth cycle, with NKT informing us of its hyperbolic growth from 2021 to 2023, with over EUR 15B of awarded projects in 2023. Another EUR 15B could materialize as firm order intake after contracts are signed. This year, we may end up well above EUR 15B, since after six months the market was already reporting around EUR 10B of awarded projects.

Eurlectric, the European association of the electricity industry, in its Action Plans on Grids informs us that more than 40% of European power grid infrastructure is over 40 years old and require an estimated EUR 584B in power grid infrastructure in this decade. But the need to drastically increase investments in grid should lead to over EUR 65B spent per year through 2050.

NKT's 1H24 results confirm this trend, as the company reported among its key highlights a 39% revenue growth (29% organic), a 48% increase in operational EBITDA and, most importantly, a high-voltage order backlog of EUR 11.3B, 7.6B of which came in during Q2.

NKT: Business Model And Structure

So, back to our company. We already gave a quick overview. Let's now look at its business model and crunch some numbers. The attached picture shows how NKT is organized. We have the total revenue above EUR 2.3B with different degrees of profitability depending on the business line.

In fact, the Solution business line shows in real terms an EBITDA margin of 13.8% while Applications is at 5.3% and Service is at 9.3%.

This proves how NKT depends on high-voltage cables, with lousy if not poor profitability in the other areas of the company (maybe this is the reason the CEO was changed in 2023). On the other end, this shows also the brilliant perspective of the Company, since with the existing order book and sales in the segment that I estimate to be around EUR 2B per year over the next 5-6 years, the most profitable area of business is already fully loaded. It is also mentioned in the Annual Report that with this favorable market condition the company will improve its contractual margin generating better financial results working more on price side than in volume. After all, NKT's production capacity seems fully saturated for many years, giving the company the strength to increase pricing. Additionally, in 2024, NKT disinvested from the photonic business and acquired a Portuguese company called SolidAl showing determination to concentrate only on energy cable and accessories.

NKT: Recent Financials

As the company is used to report number based on standard material costs (Revenue at standard metal prices – Revenue at standard metal prices for copper and aluminum) it is useful to add also some figures in real terms, showing that NKT does flex some pricing power when commodity prices go up.

NKT uses to show its numbers based both on standard metal price and on market metal price. Its numbers based on standard metal price show the real organic growth not inflated by the ups and downs of the metal price. The financial numbers based on market value, on the other hand, show the real results of the company and its ability to charge customers in case of sharp increases of raw material costs. These, at the end, are the numbers that generate real cash and return for shareholders.

Let's take a look at the income statement from 2019 through 2023.

Looking at total revenues and EBITDA, as showed in the table reported above, NKT has improved from a non-existing EBITDA in 2019 to a 10% EBITDA margin in 2023. At the end of 1H24, it increased to 10.7%, thanks to the Solution business line EBITDA margin of 15%, while Application was at 6.2% and Service & Accessories at 8%.

We have already talked about the growing backlog. But in 1H24, although Q2 was particularly strong, we see the backlog stabilizing vs Q4 2023. This could be a sign of demand stabilization, at very high levels, nonetheless.

In any case, the order backlog provides strong visibility till the end of this decade. Moreover, we need to remember that another EUR 2.5B in capacity reservations are not included in the backlog. This leads us to two consequences: free cash flow and production capacity.

NKT's cash flow shows a positive development in 2023 vs 2022. The main drive was the positive impact, in addition to improved EBITDA margins, of contract liabilities (comprising contractual unconditional invoicing for projects not yet executed), with a positive impact on cash flow of EUR 359M. As NKT's CFO stated: “The nature of the high-voltage market means that Solutions will normally be able to improve its working capital position when the order backlog increases“.

We see this in the H1 interim report. The order backlog increases, therefore, customers pay the advance payment which is recorded as a liability on the balance sheet. But this improves the working capital because it is calculated as current assets minus current liabilities. An improvement in working capital therefore is recorded, as we can see in the cash flow statement highlights below:

In addition, a capital increase by rights issue was successfully completed in July 2023. All new shares offered under the rights issue were subscribed for and this generated gross proceeds of EUR 368M, which NKT received in Q3 2023. Now, I am a bit puzzled on why the company chose to raise capital in this way, given the strong and increasing backlog we have talked about. It seems the company was not expecting an incredibly strong 2023 when it though about this capital increase. In any case, it helped the company finance its investments, but it doesn't seem the most efficient and cheapest way to do so, given how the market is doing.

There are talks about the possibility of a dividend next year, with a distribution of around 33% of the company's net income. A buyback could also be considered. But the conservative management of the company's cash leaves me a bit in doubt regarding a generous dividend policy from NKT.

The company's investments are mainly on capacity expansion and were €236M and at year-end 2023. NKT had total available liquidity reserves of EUR 1.09B, comprising cash of EUR 890M and undrawn credit facilities of EUR 200M.

Finally I have to mention R&D investment at stable level in 2022 and 2023 at around EUR 37M, showing that innovation is not a big topic in this business. What will play a decisive role for the next 5-10 years won't be technological innovation, but production capacity to fulfill a growing demand for high-voltage cables.

In fact, a real issue is that strong demand for high-voltage DC cables has saturated the production capacity of NKT and most of its peers.

Exceptional Order Book is giving visibility on the next 5-7 year on the most profitable area of NKT.

Valuation

In my opinion, over the years NKT capitalized on the increase in orders and saturated its production lines. This has clearly helped the company in improving its financials. From now on, the results can only improve if they flex their pricing power, since, as far as I can read the available data, NKT is fully saturated until the end of this decade.

Among its weaknesses, there is a strong dependence on a single German customer. Moreover, in the Application business, profitability is low. In fact, low-voltage cables can be considered commodities, and price competition is strong. Sooner or later, NKT will either take strong cost-cutting actions or sell the business line. For the next few years, NKT will likely post improving results and a small bet on the company may be reasonable. However, right now, the company seems to rely mainly on the market's own strength and doesn't seem to be able to steal market share and expand further. As a result, it makes sense that it trades at lower multiples compared to the global leader Prysmian (29 PE vs. 38). As far as I see it, NKT, though enjoying slightly better margins than Prysmian, trades at a discount vs. the leader because investors are starting to realize that NKT's growth for some years won't rely on expanding production, but only on pricing. This carries some issues with it, as we can imagine. In fact, most analysts expect NKT's revenue growth to moderate from the low 20s to high-single-digits by next year. All in all, the company seems fairly priced right now and without proving it can expand rapidly its production capacity, we are more or less in a situation where we can expect it to grow a bit above the rate of inflation. This is why my pick stays Prysmian, since it has more room to expand its operations and has a much wider global reach. NKT can be rated as a buy for many of the reasons I explained above, but since I am not putting my money on the stock, I am rating it as a hold, accordingly to the neutral stance I am taking.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Diversified Energy: The Risks Of Asset Retirement Obligations Are Too Great For Investors
data.description:
Thesis

Diversified Energy (OTCQX:DEC) faces a significant cost for retiring its wells, and the business at current natural gas prices will not have the resources to meet this obligation and provide investors a healthy return. Timing of well retirement is sensitive to energy prices; higher energy prices allow low production wells to operate for longer economically and thus delay the need to retire them. This allows them to generate more profits whilst they scale up their plugging business. Facing the business is a lot of uncertainty yet the derivatives book does mitigate this substantially, thus providing a potential investment case for a high-yield stock. However, the downside is too great in my opinion, with the risk of substantial capital loss if profitability remained low, leading to accelerated plugging.

Business Overview

The production output of shale gas follows a harmonic decline curve, which is to say a very fast initial rate of decline followed by more stable output. Most producers focus on the first few years of output, leaving many years of far less significant but stable production. Diversified Energy’s business model focuses on these low-decline wells, acquiring them from other energy companies. Diversified does not engage in exploration, but management of these wells over their lifetime, which includes optimisation and retirement of the wells. This business model of operating many low production wells has made the business the operator of the most shale wells in the United States.

When production ceases, a well can leak methane. This is why there is regulation requiring companies to retire wells safely. Naturally, Diversified does a lot of this and thus has a significant well retirement business. Abandoned wells, those left by companies in bankruptcy, are the obligation of the state to close, and Diversified’s well retirement business services some of these. But as we will see later, an accurate estimate of the retirement cost obligations encumbering the company is key to determining the risk and value to investors.

Well Retirement Obligations

Diversified Energy Company, overseeing an expansive portfolio of aging natural gas wells, confronts substantial asset retirement obligations (AROs). Although the inception of their well retirement enterprise, Next LVL, signifies progress, the financial liabilities linked to these assets may be significantly undervalued on their balance sheet. According to company disclosures, AROs are reported at a discounted value based on the presumption that well retirements will occur in the distant future, thereby mitigating immediate financial repercussions.

However, this accounting approach may considerably understate the true costs of well decommissioning. Diversified estimates an average retirement cost of approximately $23,000 per well, yet industry standards suggest this figure is low. Such a discrepancy raises investor concerns that the company's fiscal liabilities might be profoundly underestimated.

Modifications in key assumptions could significantly affect investor interests. If regulatory pressures intensify or environmental regulations become more stringent, Diversified might be compelled to retire wells sooner than anticipated, thereby inflating costs beyond current estimates. A decline in energy prices could render certain wells economically unviable, pressuring the company to decommission them earlier. Faced with limited capacity to retire wells, Diversified may persist in operating loss-making wells to postpone retirement expenses—a strategy fraught with long-term risks.

The discount rate utilised to calculate AROs is equally critical. Currently set at about 3.7%, a decrease in this rate—highly possible in a declining interest rate environment—would amplify the present value of future retirement costs. A reduction to 3% could elevate the obligation by $924 million, according to the half-year report, roughly 1.6 times the company's market capitalization.

Additionally, inflation assumptions pegged between 2% and 3% I think are underestimated due to escalating government debt and stagnating economies leading to a need for debt monetisation (printing money). Intensifying geopolitical tensions could drive costs upward. Consequently, actual retirement expenditures may exceed projections.

For investors, these considerations pose substantial risks. Deferring retirement costs preserves short-term cash flow and profitability, but transfers significant risk into the future. Changes in industry regulations, market conditions, or economic factors such as inflation and discount rates could bring these issues to the forefront sooner than expected, potentially affecting the company's liquidity and necessitating additional capital. Investors seeking long-term stability should view this as a significant red flag, while even those with shorter-term horizons should remain vigilant regarding the associated risks.

Is Diversified Avoiding Plugging Costs?

One criticism is that the company is avoiding the costs of plugging wells that are not economical to operate. Management could be trying to pay as much in dividends as possible before defaulting on its obligations, in which case it becomes an obligation of the state to retire abandoned wells.

Let’s take an example. In 2018, approximately 13,000 wells were acquired. And they produced a total of 53,000 mcf/day. That’s 4.08 mcf each day per well – not a very high figure. If these declined at 5% a year, they would now be producing less than 3 mcf a day on average. At today’s prices, many of these wells likely make a loss, but at $23,000 to plug a well it would take approximately $300 million to retire. You will see later that the total obligation dwarves actual plugging expenditures.

A study was conducted by the Ohio River Valley Institute on the company’s operations and whether or not they are sustainable.

“Diversified claims it can plug wells at a cost less than half the industry average. They claim their dying wells will continue producing at an economic rate for decades.” ... “These unusual assumptions—as well as accounting practices that function to punt cleanup costs down the line—are not used by any other company in the industry.”

The Derivative Book

The company’s derivative book is more positive for investors and could suggest the company is trading below net asset value, but only if you accept the value of the well retirement obligation. The latest reports show a net liability of $612 million in its derivative portfolio, representing the fair value of future payments expected under its hedging contracts. These obligations arise because current natural gas prices exceed the fixed prices established in the company’s swaps, collars, and other derivative instruments. Yet expected prices over the term of the derivatives come from the forward price curve, as determined by the market (seen above).

The negative net value of the obligation is therefore a positive because it is expected that energy prices arise, which will make the core business far more profitable. As we have seen in recent years, due to the high ratio of fixed to variable costs in the low decline well business, operational performance is highly sensitive to the price of energy. Only 75% of expected natural gas production is hedged by the company, so profitability from increases in price will likely outweigh obligations on the derivative book. This is subject, of course, to their operational risk of production level. But because operations are spread across so many small wells, which have more predictable production volumes, production is more stable. If higher energy prices were not realised for some reason, then the obligations to derivative counterparties would be lower than expected.

And so this represents a potential opportunity, at least a strong positive in terms of value. Currently, the company is trading roughly in line with the net book value recorded on its balance sheet. Because the positive side of the hedge – the profits from higher prices – are not recorded anywhere, it creates a situation where there is significant upside from the record. But this will be offset to some degree by the company’s well retirement obligation, which, as I have previously covered, may be a great understatement. The trouble is that if oil prices decline, whilst the company’s profitability is largely hedged, the accelerated cost of plugging its wells is not, which could lead to financial trouble. The variability in magnitude of the possible outcomes is thus substantial.

Cash flows

Free Cash Flow (before interest and asset retirement obligations): Cashflow from operating activities has increased steadily as a result of their hedging activities. Because of the business model, free cash flow should take into account acquisitions; they do not explore and drill new wells, and thus, to remain a long-term business, acquisitions are a part of their model. Once this is taken into account, free cash flow is far more mediocre. The last analyst to cover this stock on Seeking Alpha didn’t point this out and used what, I believe, is a highly inflated free cash flow figure. So if the business wants to continue as it is, replacing (and growing) proved reserves, then the cash that goes to interest and shareholders and that can be used to scale up asset retirement must come after this.

Dividend Sustainability: I separated out interest and asset retirement obligations from the free cash flow before to show you how the cashflow must be used to meet both these obligations and also pay dividends. Cash paid for interest on its own already exceeds the cashflow after acquisitions, which means their strategy incorporates increasing debt. Let’s say the company retired 5% of their 70,000 wells a year at $23,000, as they have stated their cost is, but which is less than the industry figure. That’s approximately $80m a year. Do the dividends look sustainable to you?

Conclusion

The continual payment of dividends and returns to shareholders relies on increasing energy prices, which is essentially the point of this article – an investment in this stock is like a highly leveraged bet on energy prices. If energy prices increase substantially, then many of their wells will be able to continue production economically, thus delaying the plugging cost and allowing dividends to be paid out to investors. They may be able to continue over the long term. If natural gas prices remain near the lows they are at, however, because they are not fully hedged, they may be able to avoid operational losses, but they will be barely profitable, and unable to meet their significant obligations whilst also generating a return for shareholders. This differs from other energy businesses, which, if they are healthy, can operate throughout the cycle and over the long term provide returns to shareholders whilst defending capital values. Additional risk could come from their inability to refinance, although in a decreasing rate environment this is less of a risk. In my opinion, the risks to capital values are far too great and not compensated for enough by the high yield and potential for appreciation, and that is why my call is a sell.

data.title: BitFuFu: Cloud Mining Is Powering Growth
data.description:
I initiated coverage on BitFuFu (NASDAQ:FUFU) in June. That article, titled: BitFuFu: The Lesser-Known Bitcoin Miner With Growth Potentials, was the first coverage of the stock here on Seeking Alpha. I went into detail about the company's business model and operations, and the Q1 financial results in that coverage.

Following my initial coverage on June 6 at a stock price of $3.59, FUFU has seen a volatile price movement, mainly due to Bitcoin’s volatility in recent times. Shortly after that publication, FUFU surged by 86%, reaching $6.71 two weeks later on June 25. Following that initial price surge, FUFU witnessed a series of volatility, recording a high of around $5.80 in July and a low of $3.90 the same month. FUFU currently trades at $4.60. We’ll find out in this article if FUFU is currently undervalued relative to peers and if the current price would be a good buy for the stock.

For readers not familiar with the company, BitFuFu is a Bitcoin (BTC-USD) mining company headquartered in Singapore. The company has close ties with Bitcoin mining hardware manufacturing giant BITMAIN, an existing partnership with one of the biggest Bitcoin mining pools in the world ANTPOOL, and has a founder and CEO who is very experienced in the crypto mining business. The founder and CEO Leo Lu headed the cloud mining division at BITMAIN for several years, before going on to found BitFuFu in December 2020. Several other key members of BitFuFu’s team were former members at BITMAIN and Bitdeer (BTDR).

Leo Lu’s experience in cloud mining from his time at BITMAIN reflects on BitFuFu operations today. Cloud mining has been an innovative business line for BitFuFu and is becoming the main growth driver for the company, now accounting for about 60% of the company's total revenue as of Q2 2024.

FUFU started trading on Nasdaq in March this year, following a successful business combination with Arisz Acquisition in a $1.5 billion deal.

A Look At BitFuFu Q2 Financial and Operational Highlights

BitFuFu released their unaudited Q2 results on August 20. The financial result showed increase in sales, surge in cloud mining users, and impressive sales record from the cloud mining segment. For self-mined BTC production, the total BTC mined from the self-mining operations decreased 23.1% YoY, from 1,014 BTC in Q2 2023 to 780 BTC in Q2 2024. This decline in BTC produced during the quarter doesn't come as a surprise as virtually every Bitcoin miner has recorded a decline in BTC production since the Bitcoin halving event in April.

Zooming out and examining BitFuFu’s revenue trend, BitFuFu's total revenue has grown at an impressive ~66% CAGR since 2021. In the latest financial result, BitFuFu recorded $129.4 million in total revenue for Q2 2024 - which is a 69.7% YoY increase and a noteworthy feat, considering the effects of the April 2024 halving event on the mining industry. BitFuFu’s full-year revenue in 2021 was $103 million. In 2022 revenue grew by 92%, bringing the full-year 2022 revenue to $198 million. In 2023 revenue grew by 43%, reaching $284.1 million for the full year.

BitFuFu is already on track to surpass 2023 full-year $284.1 million revenue by a significant margin because the revenues recorded so far in Q1 2024 and Q2 2024 totals $273.8 million, which already nearly matches the 2023 full-year revenue. With such performance in the first half of 2024 alone, if we extrapolate the sales figure for the year we can make a valid assumption that BitFuFu is on track to maintain the same impressive 66% CAGR or even higher for the 2024 fiscal year. In our extrapolation, we still have to keep risk factors like market volatility and macro factors at the back of our minds. With these factors in mind, and assuming other factors remain constant, I believe that this assumption isn't far-fetched. Historically, BitFuFu has recorded slightly higher revenue in the second half of the year compared to the first half, which is in line with this revenue extrapolation.

On the liquidity front, BitFuFu has managed its balance sheet impressively. BitFuFu has achieved one of the best fleet efficiencies without a heavy balance sheet leverage, and has consecutively maintained a positive net asset since 2022, and still maintained a positive net asset in Q2 2024, despite the impact of the April 2024 halving on the mining industry. Total assets in Q2 2024 was $293.2 million. Total liabilities was $175.43 million, resulting in a net asset of $117.77 million. Cash, cash equivalents, and digital assets held by BitFuFu at the end of Q2 was $155 million, implying a healthy short-term and long-term liquidity position.

BitFuFu’s cloud mining business model is an advantage for BitFuFu's ongoing liquidity. The cloud mining segment provides BitFuFu with active working capital as users subscribe to Bitcoin cloud mining services. As users pay upfront to subscribe to cloud mining services, it generates immediate cash inflow for BitFuFu, which can be used to fund ongoing operations.

Since Q2 2022, BitFuFu has scaled its hosting capacity by more than 500%, which was at 100 MW in Q1 2022 and at 522 MW as of the end of Q2. BitFuFu’s mining capacity has also grown from 3.0 EH/S in Q1 2022 to 24.7 EH/S as of the end of Q2. BitFuFu's mining capacity growth has been matched with improved fleet efficiency. BitFuFu's mining fleet has recorded reduced Joules per Terahash (J/Th) values over the quarters stands at ~21.1 J/Th. This is proof of BitFuFu's expertise in scaling resources to achieve better efficiency. At an average fleet efficiency of 21.1 J/Th, BitFuFu currently boasts one of the lowest joules per terahash value (best fleet) in the industry. The joules per terahash metric simply shows how much electricity a miner’s fleet consumes per terahash of hashrate. The less electricity consumed, the better it is for the miner’s operations. Hence, lower Joules per terahash means better efficiency.

How BitFuFu Stacks Up to Peers

In terms of production capacity and efficiency, BitFuFu stacks up high in the industry. As mentioned earlier, BitFuFu’s average fleet efficiency stands at around 21.1 J/Th, surpassing larger mining peers in terms of efficiency. MARA Holdings (former Marathon Digital) (MARA) currently averages 23.5 J/Th in efficiency, Bitdeer sees 31.6 J/Th in efficiency, and Hut 8 (HUT) has an average efficiency of 31.7 J/Th.

From a relative valuation standpoint, BitFuFu currently trades at 1.82x P/S ratio and 1.72x EV/Sales. Peers like MARA trade at 7.98x P/S ratio and 9.55x EV/Sales. HUT trade at 3.49x P/S and 4x EV/Sales. A cloud mining peer BTDR trades at 2.31x P/S and 2.92x EV sales. FUFU is currently undervalued to the peer group based on its sales figure, and if we further go by the extrapolated value for full year 2024 potential sales of ~$547 million, forward P/S sales and EV/sales for BitFuFu would be 1.37x and 1.47x respectively. This would make FUFU one of the most undervalued stocks among Bitcoin miners.

Risks

As reiterated on Bitcoin stocks covered on Seeking Alpha, crypto assets and stocks linked to crypto exhibit a higher amount of price instability. Volatility in Bitcoin's price can also affect the financial standing of mining companies as well, as they typically hold large amounts of Bitcoin on their balance sheet.

Takeaway

Revisiting FUFU, a “buy” is recommended for this mining stock for investors interested in the Bitcoin mining sector because BitFuFu has maintained its track record of strong operational and financial performance. BitFuFu has plenty of room to scale its operations and grow its business, and its business model allows for such expansion with ease. The cloud mining user base growth, currently 395,000+ registered cloud mining users, bodes well for the company as it provides active working capital for the company when users purchase mining contracts, reducing the need to leverage its balance sheet.

With the growing interest in gaining crypto exposure among retail and institutional investors, BitFuFu could turn out to be the star mining company of this Bitcoin market cycle, mainly because of cloud mining.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Northrim BanCorp: Rosy Earnings Outlook Appears Priced-In
data.description:
Interest rate cuts will boost the margin and the loan portfolio of Northrim BanCorp, Inc. (NASDAQ:NRIM) in upcoming quarters, which will, in turn, lift the earnings. I’m expecting the company to report earnings of $5.97 per share for 2024, up 33% year-over-year, and $6.04 per share for 2025, up by just 1%. The December 2025 target price is below the current market price. Further, the company is offering a moderate dividend yield of 3.5%. Based on the total expected return, I’m adopting a hold rating on Northrim BanCorp.

Loan Growth Likely to Remain Stable

Northrim BanCorp’s loan portfolio grew by 4.8% in the first half of 2024, or 9.7% annualized, which is below last year’s unusually high growth rate of 19.1%. In my opinion, loan growth won’t slow down any further partly because of the oil industry, which is the driver of Alaska’s economy.

The management appeared positive about loan growth in the earnings presentation because of future oil development projects. The biggest of these investments include Santos and Repsol’s $2.6 billion investment in Alaska’s Pikka Oil Field and ConocoPhillips’ $8 billion investment in Willow, AK. Moreover, the Alaska North Slope crude oil price is currently stable at an above-average level. The strength of the oil and gas industry indirectly affects NRIM’s loan growth in other industries as well because Alaska’s economy is heavily reliant on oil.

The state’s unemployment rate is currently worse compared to the last couple of years, which makes me think that the loan growth during my forecast horizon will be unable to return to the historical 5-year CAGR of 12.7%. Further, the coincident index has been kind of flattish in recent months.

Residential mortgages make up 16% of total loans. The outlook for this segment is bright because of interest rate cuts, which will boost the demand for mortgages because borrowing costs are usually a deciding factor for home purchases. Further, the home price index has become flatter this year, which bodes well for housing affordability and consequently for credit demand.

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the loan portfolio to grow by 2.25% every quarter through the end of 2025. Further, I’m expecting deposits to grow in line with loans. The following table shows my balance sheet estimates.

Financial Position FY19 FY20 FY21 FY22 FY23 FY24E FY25E
Net Loans 1,024 1,423 1,402 1,488 1,772 1,943 2,124
Growth of Net Loans 6.2% 38.9% (1.5)% 6.1% 19.1% 9.6% 9.3%
Other Earning Assets 429 506 1,157 987 814 750 757
Deposits 1,372 1,825 2,422 2,387 2,485 2,576 2,816
Borrowings and Sub-Debt 19 25 36 34 24 55 55
Common equity 207 222 238 219 235 266 296
Book Value Per Share ($) 30.4 34.5 38.1 37.5 41.5 47.9 53.3
Tangible BVPS ($) 28.1 32.0 35.5 34.8 38.6 45.0 50.4

Source: SEC Filings, Analyst's Estimates

(In USD million unless otherwise specified)

Further Margin Expansion Ahead

Northrim BanCorp’s net interest margin continued to expand by 18 basis points in the first half of 2024 after surging by 29 basis points in 2023 and 27 basis points in 2022. The management is confident about further margin expansion. It mentioned in the presentation that it expects new and repricing loans to drive the net interest margin.

Further, interest rate cuts will lift the margin as more deposits than loans will re-price within a year. As mentioned in the presentation, 28% of loans will mature or reprice in three months and 16% of loans will mature or reprice in three to twelve months. This means the rates on 56% of loans will remain fixed in a year’s time.

In contrast, a lower proportion of deposits will retain their cost level one year out. Non-interest-bearing deposits make up 29% of total deposits, and time deposits make up 17% of total deposits. This means the rates on at most 46% of deposits will remain fixed in a year’s time. Compare this to 56% of loans.

I’m expecting the Fed to cut rates by 25-50 basis points in the fourth quarter of 2024 and 75-100 basis points in 2025. Considering these factors, I’m expecting the net interest margin to grow by four basis points in the second half of 2024 and twelve basis points in 2025.

Expecting EPS of $5.97 in 2024, $6.04 in 2025

The anticipated loan growth and margin expansion will drive earnings in the remainder of 2024 and 2025. Additionally, I’m making the following assumptions.

  1. Provisioning for expected loan losses subsided in the first half of this year to a negligible amount. I’m expecting the provisioning expense to return to the 2023 level soon.
  2. I’m expecting rate cuts to boost the mortgage market, and thereby lift the gains on sales of mortgage loans. I’m expecting these gains to return to the 2021 level by the end of 2025. I’m also expecting other non-interest income to remain stable.
  3. I’m expecting the efficiency ratio to remain rangebound near the level seen in the first half of this year.

Overall, I’m expecting earnings to grow by 33% to $5.97 per share in 2024, and then by just 1% to $6.04 per share in 2025. The following table shows my income statement estimates.

Income Statement FY19 FY20 FY21 FY22 FY23 FY24E FY25E
Net interest income 64 71 81 95 103 110 121
Provision for loan losses (1) 2 (4) 2 4 2 4
Non-interest income 37 63 52 34 26 37 41
Non-interest expense 77 89 89 89 94 102 114
Net income - Common Sh. 21 33 38 31 25 33 34
EPS - Diluted ($) 3.04 5.11 6.00 5.27 4.49 5.97 6.04

Source: SEC Filings, Earnings Releases, Analyst's Estimates

(In USD million unless otherwise specified)

Geographic Concentration Makes NRIM Risky

Northrim BanCorp is not geographically diverse as most of its operations are based in Alaska. Further, the economy of Alaska is not as diversified as the economies of most other states, and Alaska is heavily dependent on the oil industry. Therefore, I think the risk of investing in this stock is moderate.

Further, uninsured deposits totaled a sizable $980.0 million or 40% of total deposits as of June 30, 2024, as mentioned in the 10-Q filing.

On the other hand, the asset quality is quite good. Non-performing loans totaled $4.8 million at the end of June, which is just 0.26% of total loans. Further, gross unrealized losses on the Available-for-Sale securities portfolio amounted to only $21.6 million. To put this number in perspective, $21.6 million is just 9% of the total equity book value.

Offering a Dividend Yield of 3.5%

Northrim BanCorp is offering a dividend yield of 3.5%, assuming the company maintains its quarterly dividend at the current level of $0.62 per share. The earnings and dividend estimates suggest a payout ratio of 41% for 2025, which is higher than the five-year average of 36%. As a dividend payout of 41% is easily sustainable, I don’t think there is any threat of a dividend cut. Moreover, the company reported a total risk-based capital ratio of 12.58%, which is comfortably higher than the minimum regulatory requirement of 10.0%, as mentioned in the 10-Q filing. Therefore, NRIM does not need to cut back on dividends to build up capital.

Adopting a Hold Rating

I last released a report on Northrim BanCorp in March 2023 wherein I adopted a hold rating with a December 2023 target price of $50.4. NRIM’s stock price closed at $57.21 on December 29, 2023, so I wasn’t too off in my previous analysis. So far in 2024, the stock price has rallied by a massive 24.75%. The question is, is there any upside still left after the rally?

I’m using the historical price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) and price-to-earnings (“P/E”) multiples to value Northrim BanCorp. The stock has traded at an average P/TB ratio of 1.319x and an average P/E ratio of around 9.628x in the past, as shown below.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with the forecast tangible book value per share of $50.4 gives a target price of $66.5 for the end of 2025. This price target implies a 6.9% downside from the October 18 closing price. The following table shows the sensitivity of the target price to the P/TB ratio.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with the forecast earnings per share of $6.04 gives a target price of $58.1 for the end of 2025. This price target implies an 18.6% downside from the October 18 closing price. The following table shows the sensitivity of the target price to the P/E ratio.
